articles.title: You Don't Need a Ferrari to Get Groceries: Choosing the Right AI Architecture
articles.excerpt: How we built a medical document engine that runs on a $300 GPU, and why the temptation to throw a massive LLM at every problem is the real engineering failure.
articles.author: Amphibian Labs
body: ## The Trap

There is a pattern I see constantly in AI-driven projects. A team needs to extract structured data from documents: patient names, dates, medications, lab results. The first instinct? Spin up a 70B-parameter model, feed it the raw text, and pray the JSON comes back clean.

It works. Kind of. The model understands context, handles edge cases, and produces reasonable output. But it also consumes 40+ GB of VRAM, responds in seconds instead of milliseconds, costs real money per request if hosted in the cloud, and becomes a single point of failure for the entire system.

This is the equivalent of buying a Ferrari to drive to the grocery store two blocks away. It will get you there, but the fuel cost, the maintenance, and the overkill are absurd. What you needed was a Ford Fiesta.
## The Problem We Were Solving

DocIQ is a medical document processing engine. It takes PDFs, prescriptions, lab results, clinical histories, and extracts structured, validated data from them. It supports English and Spanish. Some documents are text-based, others are scanned images that need OCR. The output must be clean enough to map to FHIR R4 resources for healthcare interoperability.
The naive approach would be: take the entire PDF, convert it to text, send it to GPT-4 or DeepSeek-V3 or Llama 70B, and ask it to return JSON. One API call, done.
Here is why we did not do that.
## What a Big Model Actually Gives You (and What It Doesn't)

Large language models are generalists. They can write poetry, explain quantum mechanics, and extract a patient name from a prescription, all in the same session. That generality is their strength and their cost.

When you use a 70B model to extract a date from a medical document, you are paying for the model's ability to also discuss philosophy. That capability is not free. It lives in billions of parameters that need to be loaded into memory, computed through, and cooled.

For a structured extraction task with well-defined fields and predictable document layouts, most of that capacity is wasted. You are not asking the model to reason about ambiguous prose. You are asking it to find the string next to 'Patient Name:' and put it in a JSON field.
## The Architecture We Built Instead
Instead of one monolithic model doing everything, we built a pipeline of specialized stages, each optimized for its specific job:

**1. PDF Extraction: PyMuPDF (no model at all).** Text-based PDFs do not need AI. PyMuPDF reads the text layer directly: instant, deterministic, zero compute overhead. We only invoke heavier tools when we actually need them.

**2. OCR: PaddleOCR (PP-OCRv4).** For scanned documents and images, we use PaddleOCR. It is a CNN-based OCR engine that runs on CPU. No GPU required. It handles Latin-script languages well, and while it strips accents from Spanish text, we compensate with accent-flexible regex patterns in our extractors. The point is: OCR is a solved problem. We do not need a language model to read pixels into characters.

**3. Classification: Keyword Scoring (no model at all).** To determine whether a document is a prescription, a lab result, or a clinical history, we use a weighted keyword scorer. Words like 'dosage,' 'frequency,' and 'receta medica' push the score toward prescription. Words like 'findings,' 'impression,' and 'hallazgos' push toward result. This runs in microseconds, never fails to load, and is trivially debuggable. When it is wrong, you add a keyword and re-run. Try debugging a misclassification in a 70B model.

**4. NER Extraction: Rule-Based Extractors.** Each document type has its own extractor with regex patterns and structural heuristics tuned for that specific format. The prescription extractor knows how to find medication names, dosages, and frequencies. The result extractor knows how to find findings and impressions. These are deterministic, fast, and testable. We have over 1,100 tests covering edge cases.

**5. Validation: Pydantic Schemas.** Every extracted result passes through strict Pydantic v2 schemas. If a field is missing or malformed, the validation layer catches it before anything downstream ever sees it.
**6. LLM Enhancement: Qwen 2.5 3B via Ollama (optional, opt-in).** Here is the key design decision: the LLM is not the engine. It is the safety net.

We use qwen2.5:3b, a 3-billion parameter model running locally through Ollama. It is configured with a temperature of 0.1 for near-deterministic output and a 4096-token context window. It fits comfortably on an RTX 3050 with 4 GB of VRAM.

The LLM only activates when explicitly enabled. When it does, the merge strategy is clear: rule-based values always win. The LLM only fills gaps where the regex extractors could not find a value. If the rule-based system found the patient's name, the LLM's opinion is ignored.
And if Ollama is unreachable? The pipeline does not break. It silently falls back to rule-based results. The LLM is an enhancement, not a dependency.
## The Right Tool Principle
This is the core lesson: match the tool to the task, not the task to the tool.

A 3B model running locally on a budget GPU can handle the specific, constrained work of filling in gaps that regex missed. It does not need to understand Shakespeare or generate creative fiction. It needs to look at medical text and find a date. For that job, 3 billion parameters is more than enough.

Meanwhile, the heavy lifting, text extraction, OCR, classification, NER, is done by specialized, deterministic components that are faster, cheaper, more predictable, and easier to debug than any language model.
The pipeline still works with zero GPU. The LLM is a bonus, not a requirement.
## Why Teams Fall into This Trap
It is not laziness. It is a rational shortcut that becomes irrational at scale.

When you are prototyping, sending text to a large model and getting back JSON is fast to develop. You skip the work of writing extractors, designing classification logic, defining schemas. The model handles the ambiguity for you. That is a legitimate advantage during exploration.

The problem is when the prototype becomes the architecture. Suddenly you are scaling a system where every request needs a GPU-second of inference, where failures are opaque, and where the cost scales linearly with volume.

The engineering discipline is in knowing when to stop leaning on the generalist and start building the specialist. A well-designed pipeline with deterministic components and an optional LLM fallback is harder to build upfront. But it is cheaper to run, easier to test, simpler to debug, and more predictable in production.
## The Counterargument (and When Big Models Make Sense)

To be fair: there are absolutely cases where a large model is the right choice. If your input is genuinely unstructured, free-form clinical notes with no predictable layout, multilingual documents with inconsistent formatting, or tasks requiring deep semantic reasoning, then a larger model earns its keep.

The question is not 'are big models good?' They are extraordinary. The question is: does your specific problem require that level of generality? If you are extracting structured data from semi-structured documents with known layouts, the answer is almost certainly no.
## Takeaways

**Design the architecture before choosing the model.** If you start by picking the model, you will design the system around its capabilities and constraints. If you start by designing the pipeline, you will pick the model that fits.
**Use deterministic components for deterministic tasks.** Regex does not hallucinate. Keyword scoring does not need a GPU. Pydantic validation never has a bad inference day.
**Make the LLM optional.** If your system breaks when the model is unavailable, you have built a dependency, not an enhancement. The pipeline should work without it. The model should make it better.
**Right-size the model.** If a 3B model can do the job, do not use a 70B model. The extra 67 billion parameters are not free. They cost memory, latency, energy, and money.

**Test the boring parts.** We have 1,100+ tests. Most of them test regex extractors, keyword scoring, and schema validation. These are not glamorous, but they are the reason the system works reliably in production without constant babysitting.
You do not need a Ferrari to get groceries. Sometimes the best engineering decision is choosing a reliable car and spending the savings on better brakes.

This article reflects lessons learned while building DocIQ, an open-source medical document processing engine. The project runs on an RTX 3050 with 4 GB of VRAM and processes prescriptions, lab results, and clinical histories into FHIR-compatible JSON.

articles.title: Why we enhance your systems instead of replacing them
articles.excerpt: Most businesses don't need a full platform migration. They need the systems they already trust to exchange data, automate manual steps, and expose information at the right time.
articles.author: Amphibian Labs
body: Every vendor pitch starts the same way: 'Let us show you how our platform replaces everything you currently use.' It sounds clean. It sounds modern. It almost never works as neatly as promised.
The rip-and-replace model assumes your existing systems are the problem. In many businesses, they are not.

Your CRM may work. Your accounting software may work. Your project management tool may work. Your SharePoint folders may be messy but useful. Your spreadsheets may hold operational knowledge that nobody has formally documented.
What usually fails is not the individual tool. It is the gap between tools.

That gap becomes manual work: copy-paste workflows, duplicate data entry, email attachments, spreadsheet imports, repeated reports, and information that lives in someone's inbox instead of the system of record.
At Amphibian Labs, we start from a different premise: your systems are assets, not liabilities. Our job is to make them work together.
## The cost of replacement
Replacing a core business system is not just a software decision. It affects staff training, data migration, process design, customer communication, reporting, integrations, and daily operations.
During that transition, productivity drops. Mistakes increase. People fall back to old habits. For small and medium-sized businesses, this disruption can be expensive and risky.
A new platform may be technically superior and still be a poor fit.
## Enhancement over replacement
Enhancement means adding the missing layer.

That might be an API integration between your CRM and accounting software. It might be a Power Automate workflow that removes admin work. It might be an AI backend that reads incoming documents and extracts structured data. It might be a dashboard that gives managers visibility without asking staff for another spreadsheet.
The insight is simple: most businesses do not need entirely new systems. They need their current systems to communicate, automate, and expose data more intelligently.
## When replacement is the right call

We are not dogmatic. Sometimes a system genuinely needs replacing. It may be unsupported, insecure, impossible to integrate, or so misaligned with the business that patching around it creates more risk.
When that is the case, we will say so. But replacement should be a conclusion, not a sales tactic.
## What this means for you
If you work with Amphibian Labs, we start by understanding what already works. Then we add what is missing.
You keep the useful parts of your stack, remove the manual friction around it, and own the result when the work is done. That is the model.

articles.title: How we built DocIQ: an open-source AI engine for medical document processing
articles.excerpt: A technical deep dive into DocIQ: a Python and FastAPI document intelligence backend for OCR, classification, entity extraction, validation, and FHIR R4-compatible healthcare outputs.
articles.author: Amphibian Labs
body: DocIQ started with a practical frustration. A healthcare workflow depended on PDFs: pathology reports, clinical notes, referral letters, and other documents that contained important structured information trapped inside inconsistent layouts.
The data was present, but not usable. Admin staff were reading documents, finding values, checking context, and manually entering information into another system.
That is exactly the kind of work software should support. So we built DocIQ.
## Architecture
DocIQ is a Python-based AI backend built on FastAPI. The pipeline has four main stages: ingestion, layout analysis, entity extraction, and output formatting.

**Ingestion** - Documents enter through a REST API. The system detects whether a PDF contains a usable text layer. Native PDFs are parsed directly. Scanned documents are sent through OCR. This avoids treating every document the same way and improves both speed and accuracy.

**Layout analysis** - Text alone is not enough. Document meaning often comes from structure: tables, headers, sections, columns, labels, values, reference ranges, and positional relationships. DocIQ analyses layout so extracted values preserve context.

**Entity extraction** - The extraction layer identifies the useful entities inside a document. For healthcare documents, that includes patient identifiers, test names, numeric results, units, reference ranges, clinical flags, dates, and provider information. The system combines deterministic rules, pattern matching, NLP-assisted methods, and document-specific configuration.

**Output formatting** - Extracted data is converted into structured output such as JSON. For healthcare use cases, DocIQ supports FHIR R4-compatible structures such as Patient, Observation, and DiagnosticReport-style outputs.
## Testing strategy
Document extraction systems fail quietly if they are not tested properly. That is dangerous.
DocIQ includes a broad test suite covering extraction rules, document processing flows, regression cases, and healthcare data structure expectations.
The goal is not just to extract data. The goal is to know when the system is confident, when it is uncertain, and when a human should review the result.
Every test runs in CI on every commit. No exceptions.
## Why open source?
Healthcare document processing should not only be available to large organisations with enterprise budgets.

Open source makes the system inspectable, adaptable, and improvable. It also creates trust. When a system extracts sensitive or operationally important data, people should be able to understand how it works.
## What is next?

DocIQ is expanding toward more document types, better template configuration, improved review workflows, and easier deployment for teams that need document intelligence without buying a black-box enterprise platform.
If you want to try DocIQ, it's on GitHub: github.com/pierremontanov/HealthParse

articles.title: 5 signs your business is ready for AI - and 3 signs you're not
articles.excerpt: AI succeeds when there is a clear workflow, digital data, repeatable rules, and a measurable outcome. This guide helps SMBs decide whether AI is the right next move or whether the groundwork comes first.
articles.author: Amphibian Labs
body: Every business conference, LinkedIn post, and software vendor seems to say the same thing: you need AI. Maybe you do. Maybe you don't.
AI is not magic. It is infrastructure. It works when there is a defined task, usable data, a repeatable workflow, and a measurable outcome.
Here is a practical checklist.
## 5 signs your business is ready

**1. You have a repetitive, high-volume process.** AI and automation work best when the task repeats often enough to justify the build. Examples include invoice processing, document review, report generation, email classification, customer request triage, data entry, or internal knowledge retrieval.

**2. You already have digital data.** The data doesn't need to be perfect. It can be messy PDFs, spreadsheets, emails, CRM records, scanned documents, or SharePoint folders. But it needs to exist in a format the system can access.

**3. You can define a correct outcome.** Good AI projects have clear success criteria. For example: extract these seven invoice fields, classify this document into one of five categories, flag records with missing required fields, or route this request to the right team. If the goal is 'use AI to improve things,' the project is not ready yet.

**4. You have a workflow bottleneck, not a strategy problem.** AI can help with execution problems. It can process documents, classify text, extract fields, validate records, and reduce repetitive decisions. It cannot fix an unclear business model or a team that disagrees on the process.
**5. You're willing to start small.** The best AI implementations start with one workflow, one document type, one team, or one measurable bottleneck. Prove it works. Then scale.
## 3 signs you're not ready yet

**1. Your data only exists on paper.** If the business depends on physical documents with no scanning or digital storage process, the first step is digitisation. That may include scanning, OCR, file naming, storage rules, and basic document management.

**2. Your process changes every time.** If every staff member handles the same task differently, automation will struggle. Before AI, you may need light standardisation: common fields, clear rules, and agreed exceptions.
**3. You expect a silver bullet.** AI will not fix every operational problem overnight. It works best as a targeted system: one task, one workflow, one measurable improvement at a time.
## What to do if you're not ready
Not being ready is not failure. It is information.
Digitise the documents. Standardise the process. Define success. Clean the data. Pick one workflow. Then AI has something real to work with.
Or book a discovery session and we'll help you figure out where you actually are - and what to do next.
